stem:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to your emergency department after 3 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, and 4 kg weight loss. His mother says 'he's been drinking from the tap at night and wet the bed twice last week, which he never does'. He has had no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and is eating normally. He has had no recent viral illness and no family history of autoimmune disease.

On examination he is alert, well-perfused, with moist mucous membranes and a capillary refill under 2 seconds. There is no Kussmaul breathing and his breath is not ketotic. Bedside finger-prick glucose is 22 mmol/L. Capillary blood ketones measure 0.6 mmol/L (under 0.6 normal). Venous blood gas shows pH 7.36 (7.35-7.45) and bicarbonate 22 mmol/L (22-26). Urine dipstick shows glucose 3+, ketones trace, no nitrites or leukocytes.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, mucous membranes moist, no Kussmaul breathing, no abdominal tenderness, weight tracking 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 22 mmol/L, capillary ketones 0.6 mmol/L, venous pH 7.36, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, urine glucose 3+ ketones trace

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride bolus and commence a fixed-rate insulin infusion
options.rationale: Intravenous fluids and an insulin infusion are reserved for diabetic ketoacidosis (pH under 7.3 or bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L) or significant dehydration; this child is normoglycaemic-range for new-onset diabetes without acidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Admit under the paediatric endocrinology team for subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin and structured education
options.rationale: New-onset paediatric type 1 diabetes without ketoacidosis is admitted for subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin initiation, structured family education, and arrangement of outpatient diabetes team follow-up. RCH and APEG protocols use a multi-day inpatient education pathway before discharge. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook; RCH CPG: Diabetes mellitus)

options.text: Arrange outpatient oral glucose tolerance testing and dietary review with the general practitioner in 1 week
options.rationale: OGTT is unnecessary when a child has classical symptoms and a random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L; outpatient management without insulin risks rapid progression to ketoacidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Commence empirical oral metformin and lifestyle measures pending islet autoantibody and C-peptide results
options.rationale: Metformin is the first agent for paediatric type 2 diabetes; this child has the type 1 phenotype (rapid weight loss, classical symptoms over weeks) and requires insulin. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Handbook)

options.text: Discharge with a glucose meter, finger-prick instructions, and a written safety-net to return if vomiting
options.rationale: Discharge without insulin or formal education is unsafe in newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes; vomiting after discharge would already represent decompensation. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes mellitus)

explanation.summary: New-onset paediatric type 1 diabetes without acidosis is managed by admission for subcutaneous insulin initiation and structured family education over several days. Acidosis (pH under 7.3 or bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L) defines DKA and triggers a separate fluid and infusion pathway. Diagnosis rests on random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L with symptoms; OGTT is not required when symptomatic. Islet autoantibody and C-peptide testing supports type but does not delay insulin start.
key_points: DKA threshold: pH under 7.3 OR bicarbonate under 15 mmol/L
Symptomatic plus random glucose over 11.1 mmol/L confirms diabetes - no OGTT
Subcutaneous basal-bolus initiation in absence of acidosis
Family education during admission is part of the standard pathway
explanation.pearls: Diabetic ketoacidosis fluid replacement in children uses 0.9% sodium chloride at a maintenance rate calculated over 48 hours, never as repeated large boluses, to reduce cerebral oedema risk.
